claims.text: The FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee met July 23-24, 2026 to review seven peptides for the 503A bulk list.
FDA career scientists recommended against loosening restrictions on all seven peptides.
The advisory review is non-binding and reclassification would not equal FDA drug approval.
The FDA placed 19 peptides in Category 2 in 2023; Kennedy moved 14 to a less restrictive category in February 2026.
Hims & Hers acquired a US-based peptide facility in California in February 2025.
Hims named Dr. Anant Vinjamoori CMO of Hims in June 2026 with peptide therapy in his remit, and plans to offer peptides if reclassified.
Analysts estimate a $2.2B telehealth peptide market with up to $440M for Hims; the article frames this explicitly as an analyst projection, not a measured market.
Hims FY2025 revenue was $2.3476B (+59%) with 2.511M subscribers.
Hims Q1 2026 revenue was $608.1M (+3.8% YoY) with a $92.1M net loss.
Hims received two FDA warning letters in September 2025 and was named in the February 2026 FDA statement on compounded GLP-1s.
Novo Nordisk sued Hims for patent infringement on February 9, 2026.
No human clinical studies were identified for TB-500; no human exposure data for KPV; 'insufficient evidence of effectiveness' for Semax.
The 2012 NECC fungal meningitis outbreak killed more than 60 people.
One Sermo poll of roughly 500 physicians suggests about 90% worry about patients' self-directed peptide use, as characterized by the Guardian.
Two women became critically ill after peptide injections at a 2025 Las Vegas longevity conference.
If reclassified, clinicians could prescribe the peptides beyond the reviewed indications while the products remain unapproved.
Hims began compounded injectable semaglutide in May 2024; the FDA resolved the shortage on February 21, 2025.
The industry case is harm reduction plus the patent-economics argument that these peptides will never get approval trials.
